learning_outcomes.description: understand medical textual conventions, terminology and concepts
gain knowledge of medicine and pharmaceutical development required to perform translation and interpreting in the field of health
obtain skills required to perform translation and interpreting in the field of health

assessments.assessment_title: In-Semester knowledge exam on medical domain (in-class)
assessments.task_description:
This in-class knowledge exam may include the following topics:

  • Medical terminology
  • Knowledge of Australian medical systems and processes
  • The role of the interpreter in the medical context
  • Ethics

Statement on Generative AI & MT

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Essay/ Critique, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
In this assessment, you will translate selected English medical passages into either Chinese or Japanese. The translation may be completed individually or in groups. Alongside your translations, you are required to submit a Reflective Essay that critically analyses your translation process and problem-solving strategies.

Part 1: The Translation Task

As subject-matter knowledge plays an important role in translation, you are strongly encouraged to conduct extensive background research and read widely on the assigned medical topics in both languages. Compile a list of useful background articles and other information.

While translating, be conscious of your decision-making process, including how you navigate the context of the source text, the intended audience, medical terminology, and target-language textual conventions. You will discuss those issues in translation tutorial classes. 

Part 2: The Reflective Essay

Document your translation process in a reflective essay. Your essay should explore the following three areas:

  • Issue Resolution: Identify the specific translation challenges you encountered (e.g., comprehension/contextual understanding, lexical gaps, stylistic nuances) and explain the solutions you developed.
  • Critique of Strengths & Weaknesses: Provide an appraisal of your skills (or your group's dynamics and capabilities) during the translation process.
  • Progress & Evolution: Discuss how your approach evolved. Highlight the specific ways your translation skills, research habits, or problem-solving efficiencies improved as you progressed.

Length of reflective essay: 1500 words plus or minus 10% allowance, excluding translation, lists of background materials, captions, diagrams, tables, or appendices.

Submit your translations, a list of useful references, and your reflective essay in one Word file. Note that the translation itself will not be assessed.

The essay must:

  • be typed on A4 paper (1.5 line spacing);
  • use 12-point Calibri font;
  • include any tables or diagrams in their appropriate position in the text, not at the end; and
  • use the APA referencing style. 

Statement on Generative AI & MT

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) provide emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Students will interpret two monologues in the consecutive mode: one monologue from English into Chinese/Japanese and the other from Chinese/Japanese into English. For each task, students will use the consecutive mode to interpret a monologue of about 500 words (or equivalent characters). The monologue is played from an audio recording. One week prior to the test, students will receive the topics for tasks via Blackboard for preparation. There will be no preparation time on the day of the exam.

Dictionaries, whether printed, electronic or online, and any other resources are not allowed during the exam.

Statement on Generative AI & MT

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 3(27 Jul - 16 Aug)
Multiple weeksFrom Week 4 To Week 13(17 Aug - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Translation & Interpreting practice

Interpreting tutorials:

Interpreting practice in the medical context

Translation tutorials:

You are required to translate an English passages in the medical context into Chinese or Japanese individually or in a group and facilitate a discussion in class on your decision-making processes in handling various translation-related issues, such as the context of the source text, specific terminology, and stylistic issues.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
